topic: Two strangers realize they've been dating the same person
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee shop buzzed with the low hum of conversation and the hiss of the espresso machine. Clara stared down at the latte art, her heart suddenly hammering against her ribs like a trapped bird. Across the small, worn wooden table sat a man with a familiar, slightly crooked smile. He was laughing now, a sound that scraped against her composure. When he finally looked up, his eyes met hers, and the recognition hit her with the force of a physical blow. It wasn't just a fleeting memory; it was the entire tapestry of shared inside jokes, whispered secrets, and late-night drives. A flush crept up her neck, warming her cheeks until they felt hot enough to burn. She quickly looked away, focusing intently on a sugar packet, trying to will the sensation away. The silence between them stretched, thick and suffocating, as she fought the urge to bolt from the scene.

[story 2]

Mark felt a cold dread pool in his stomach as he watched Sarah across the crowded gallery. She was leaning against a marble pillar, examining a sculpture, and the way she tilted her head, the subtle curve of her lips—it was all him. Every shared glance, every inside reference to obscure films, every perfectly timed, knowing touch. His hands tightened around his lukewarm wine glass. He wanted to melt into the floor. When she finally turned, catching his gaze, the air seemed to thin. He managed a jerky nod, a desperate attempt to appear casual, but his gaze snagged on hers, lingering just a moment too long. A tremor ran through his hands, making him grip the stem of the glass so hard his knuckles went white. He mumbled something incoherent about needing air and practically fled toward the exit, the sound of his own hurried footsteps echoing loudly in the quiet space.

[story 3]

Liam found himself frozen mid-stride in the bookstore aisle, the scent of aged paper suddenly overwhelming. He was looking for a specific edition, but his attention was entirely consumed by the woman browsing the poetry section. Her silhouette, the way she tucked a stray strand of hair behind her ear—it was an exact replication of someone he’d spent the last six months building a life around. When she turned, their eyes locked across the narrow gap of shelves. A sudden, sharp intake of breath caught in his throat. He felt a profound internal tightening, a desperate need to disappear. He shifted his weight uncomfortably, his gaze dropping instantly to the floor, his breathing becoming shallow and quick. He wanted the ground to swallow him whole, wishing the floorboards would simply give way beneath him.
